input.oneLine: Agda invokes dwarven customary law to get the party past a hill-dwarf charter holder's checkpoint; the holder's parting warning in Dwarven changes what the waystation is.

input.summary: Agda's POV. Day 4, mid-morning. The party arrives at Grenn Vott's charter checkpoint at the Hollowback's border. The booth is tidier than expected — Vott is a hill-dwarf mid-fifties who has held this gate for 22 years. He reads the party in three seconds: the Ashford contract and permits pass; Evren's papers are returned without comment; Sela's apprentice certification gets a deliberate corner-fold; Agda's enclave registration is handed back unread — no Church seal, unlicensed healer, bond required. Evren argues the Third Consolidation's public right-of-way provision; Vott counters with the Charter Enforcement Supplement, Section Four, which predates the Consolidation and specifies Church-accredited institutions. Evren runs out of angles. Agda watches Vott and begins forming a different plan. The younger employee shoves Evren; the crossbow comes up; Corvin's hand goes to his sword and stops. Agda steps in front of Evren and speaks Dwarven: names herself by clan and practitioner tradition, names Duhr, and invokes the pre-Aldenmere right of a threshold practitioner to walk border roads in service of the living. Vott pauses — the invocation has no crown-law standing and complete standing with him as a hill dwarf. The young employee doesn't wait; swings at Agda; she turns into it with her left shoulder (deltoid, not clavicle — painful, not structural). Corvin pulls the man off. The crossbow fires wide into the tree line. Vott says 'Enough.' He gives the formal three-word Dwarven acknowledgment of her invocation and says 'Pass.' Alone beside Agda before the gate, Vott warns her in Dwarven: he's watched people try the waystation for 22 years, some come back different, some don't come back, he's never gone in himself. What's in there doesn't observe the threshold correctly. He warns her specifically because a threshold practitioner will feel the distinction before anyone else. She thanks him in the formal tradition-response. On the far side of the gate, Agda tends her shoulder — invokes Duhr (short form, 'vel thanis'), swelling reduces by half, nothing miraculous. The others ask what Vott said; she translates plainly but not the full weight. Evren, unprompted, holds a lamp at the optimal angle while she works the shoulder; neither discusses it. They walk into the Hollowback. Evren is slightly closer than usual. The unlocatable water sound begins. Agda is processing what it means that the warning was addressed specifically to her. Thread T3 enters (Vott's warning about the waystation). Thread T5 advances, Evren/Agda first real beat (she steps in front of him; he holds the light).
newFacts: Grenn Vott: hill-dwarf charter holder, mid-fifties, stocky even for a dwarf, short practical beard, wears charter papers in a copper case at his belt (embossed 'crown grant'). Has held the checkpoint at this road junction for 22 years.

The checkpoint: timber gate, heavy-beamed, recently re-hung hardware (square fit, no rust at joint). Adjacent stone booth — small, kept, swept step. Window box with white-petaled small-headed winter stock flowers (practical, not decorative).

Vott's two employees at the checkpoint: older one with crossbow (positioned right, fired wide into tree line), younger one with short sword (positioned left, the one who shoved Evren and swung at Agda). Vott had not instructed the shove.
Vott's bond requirement: four marks surety against the party's return. Standard rate.
Third Consolidation: a Crown statute establishing public right-of-way provisions. Predates Vott's charter grant for this road junction by approximately 80 years. Evren had read it.

Charter Enforcement Supplement, Section Four: adopted 11 years before the Third Consolidation; therefore not superseded by it. Specifies that unlicensed healers from 'accredited institutions' (Church-specific definition) require a surety bond. This is the statutory basis for Vott's bond requirement.

Agda's dwarven customary law invocation: the pre-Aldenmere right of a threshold practitioner to walk the border roads in service of the living. She named herself by clan (Stonebrook) and practitioner tradition (four generations of threshold healers), named Duhr, and stated the old right. Has no Aldenmere crown-law standing. Had complete standing with Vott as a hill dwarf.
The formal Dwarven acknowledgment Vott gave to Agda's invocation: three-word phrase in the old register — 'I receive the claim.' Delivered in Dwarven.

Vott's parting warning (delivered privately to Agda in Dwarven, not in common): something in the waystation does not observe the threshold the way living and dead things observe it. People go in and come back different, or don't come back. He has never gone in himself — 22-year decision, no regrets. He warned Agda specifically because a threshold practitioner will feel the wrongness before others in her party will, possibly before she is ready to.
Agda gave the formal three-word tradition response to Vott's threshold warning — acknowledging that she heard him and thanked him. Delivered in Dwarven.
Agda's left shoulder: took a hit from the young employee's swing. Deltoid (not clavicle — bone intact, muscle impact). Bruise formed immediately. Full rotation present; pain on full extension.

Agda's short Duhr invocation form: 'Duhr, vel thanis' — the simple/short ask for practical healing attention. Response: warmth in the tissue, deliberate and focused, not miraculous. Swelling reduced faster than natural; rotation clean by next day. Not eliminated instantly.
Evren held a small lamp (from his person, already lit) at the optimal angle while Agda tended her shoulder — without being asked, without commenting on it. He watched the road ahead while he held it.
The party officially entered the Hollowback on Day 4, mid-morning, through Vott's checkpoint gate. After passing through, the gate was out of sight behind them within the tree line.

Vott's checkpoint and warning is the first specific warning about the waystation received in person — and the most precise: not 'stay out' but 'what is in there does not respect thresholds correctly.'
looseThreads: T3 enters: Vott's warning establishes the waystation contains something that does not observe the threshold correctly — people come back different or don't come back. The nature of this thing is unknown to Vott and unknown to the party. Pays off through Ch10–13.

T5 advances, Evren/Agda: She stepped in front of him when the fight tipped — motivated by the practical need (she was the right tool for the situation), not discussed, not acknowledged. He held the lamp without being asked, without comment. Neither spoke about either action. They walked slightly closer than usual into the Hollowback. First real beat of this pairing.

Agda did not tell the others the full weight of how Vott said the warning — specifically, the weight of his 22-year experience, or the specific phrasing about threshold practitioners. She translated the content plainly and kept the tone of it to herself. She is processing what it means that he addressed the warning to her.

Corvin's 60-second scrubline watch after the deserter ambush in Ch03: not repeated here (the situation resolved differently — Vott invited them through), but his habit continues. He watched the tree line direction of the crossbow bolt after the fight.
The crossbow bolt fired by the older employee: went into the tree line to the right. Not recovered. No injury. Empty weapon confirmed.

The young employee who shoved Evren and swung at Agda: not named. His posture after Vott's 'Enough' went somewhere behind his own face. He is not a loose thread requiring resolution but his action was not Vott's instruction — the distinction is clear.

Evren said something quiet to the young employee as he passed through the gate. Corvin made a sound that might have been the beginning of a laugh. Content of what Evren said is not known to the reader.

text: Now let me log all the new facts and update the glossary.

facts: Grenn Vott: hill-dwarf charter holder, mid-fifties, stocky even for a dwarf, short practical beard, wears charter papers in a copper case at his belt (embossed 'crown grant'). He has held the checkpoint at the eastern Hollowback road junction for 22 years. He is not corrupt, not foolish, and not wrong about the law. He is informed about the waystation and has never gone in.

The checkpoint at the Hollowback border road junction: timber gate, heavy-beamed, recently re-hung hardware (square fit, no rust at the joint). Adjacent low stone booth — swept step, window box with white-petaled, small-headed winter stock flowers (practical, not decorative). Booth is tidier than expected — someone maintains this post.

Vott's two employees at the checkpoint: older one with crossbow, positioned right; younger one with short sword, positioned left. Both position for cover, not aggression. The younger employee shoved Evren (not Vott's instruction) and swung at Agda during the confrontation. The crossbow fired wide and the bolt went into the tree line to the right.

Third Consolidation: an Aldenmere Crown statute establishing public right-of-way provisions for certain roads, including the road through Vott's charter holding. Predates Vott's charter grant for this junction by approximately 80 years. Evren had read this statute.

Charter Enforcement Supplement, Section Four: adopted 11 years before the Third Consolidation, therefore not superseded by it. Specifies that unlicensed healers from 'accredited institutions' require a surety bond. 'Accredited institution' is Church-specific in the Supplement's language. This statute is the legal basis for Vott's four-mark surety bond requirement for Agda.

Agda's dwarven customary law invocation at the checkpoint (Ch05): she named herself by clan (Stonebrook) and by practitioner tradition (four generations of threshold healers), named Duhr, and invoked the pre-Aldenmere right of a threshold practitioner to walk the border roads in service of the living. This tradition predates Aldenmere crown law. It has no standing in crown law and complete standing with Vott as a hill dwarf.

The formal Dwarven acknowledgment that Vott gave Agda's customary law invocation: three-word phrase in the old register — 'I receive the claim' — delivered in Dwarven before he said 'Pass' in common. Agda gave the formal three-word tradition response to Vott's threshold warning: she heard him, she thanked him. Both the invocation and the response are real elements of the dwarven threshold practitioner tradition.

Agda's left shoulder: struck by the young employee's swing during the Ch05 confrontation. Deltoid impacted, not clavicle (bone intact, no structural damage). Bruise formed immediately; full rotation present; pain on full extension. Treated with Agda's short Duhr invocation ('Duhr, vel thanis') on the far side of the gate. Swelling reduced faster than natural rate. Rotation clean by the following day.

Agda's short Duhr invocation form: 'Duhr, vel thanis' — the simple/short ask for practical healing attention. Response is warmth in the tissue, deliberate and focused, not miraculous or instantaneous. Established Ch05 as distinct from the three-word invocation used over the blister wrap in Ch02.

Vott's parting warning to Agda (Ch05, delivered privately in Dwarven, not translated in full to the party): something in the waystation does not observe the threshold the way living things and dead things observe it. People who enter come back different or don't come back. He has never gone in himself — a 22-year-old decision. He warned Agda specifically because a threshold practitioner will feel the wrongness before others in her party will, possibly before she is ready to feel it.
The party officially entered the Hollowback through Vott's checkpoint gate on Day 4 of travel, mid-morning. After passing through, the gate was out of sight behind them within the tree line.

Evren held a small lamp (already lit, from his person) at the optimal angle while Agda tended her left shoulder on the far side of Vott's gate — without being asked, without commenting. He watched the road ahead. Agda noticed and set it aside. This is the first Evren/Agda Thread 5 beat.

Agda translated Vott's warning to the party plainly (there is something in the waystation, people come back different or don't come back, Vott has never gone in himself) but did not communicate the full weight of how he said it, and did not specify that he addressed it to her as a threshold practitioner. She is processing this privately.

entries.definition: Hill-dwarf charter holder who controls the road checkpoint at the eastern Hollowback border junction. Mid-fifties, stocky, short practical beard, wears charter papers in a copper case at his belt. Has held the gate for 22 years. Not corrupt, not foolish, not wrong about the law. Knows the waystation exists and has never entered it. Gives Agda a private threshold warning in Dwarven before the party passes through (Ch05). Minor character with weight.

An Aldenmere Crown statute establishing public right-of-way provisions for certain roads. Predates Vott's charter grant for the Hollowback road junction by approximately 80 years. Evren had read this statute and attempted to use it to contest the surety bond requirement (Ch05).

A supplement to the Aldenmere charter statutes, adopted 11 years before the Third Consolidation and therefore not superseded by it. Section Four specifies that unlicensed healers from non-accredited (i.e., non-Church) institutions require a surety bond. The legal basis for Vott's four-mark bond requirement for Agda (Ch05).

Short-form practical Duhr invocation used by Agda: 'Duhr, vel thanis.' The simple ask for focused healing attention. Response is warmth in the tissue, accelerated but not miraculous. Distinct from the three-word invocation over the blister wrap (Ch02). Established Ch05.

A healer trained in Duhr's tradition who works the boundary between the living and dying. Agda is a threshold practitioner of the Stonebrook line, four generations back. The title has legal standing in old dwarven customary tradition and is the basis for Agda's invocation at Vott's checkpoint. Grenn Vott recognizes the title and its implications for the waystation warning.

The pre-Aldenmere dwarven customary right of a threshold practitioner to walk border roads in service of the living. Predates Aldenmere crown law and Church certification. No standing in current crown law; recognized in practice by dwarven charter holders. Invoked by Agda at Vott's checkpoint (Ch05) to resolve the surety bond dispute.

## Chapter 05 — "Charter" — complete

**~3,450 words.** Agda's POV. Tight bottle scene at the Hollowback border checkpoint.

### What the chapter does

The party arrives at **Grenn Vott's** checkpoint. Paperwork round: Agda's enclave registration is handed back unread (no Church seal = unlicensed healer = four-mark surety bond). Evren argues the **Third Consolidation**'s public right-of-way provisions; Vott counters with the **Charter Enforcement Supplement, Section Four** and wins the legal argument cleanly. Agda watches and waits.

When the younger employee shoves Evren and the crossbow comes up, Agda steps in front of Evren and switches to Dwarven — invoking the pre-Aldenmere right of a threshold practitioner to walk border roads in service of the living. It has no crown-law standing. It has complete standing with Vott. The young employee swings anyway (Agda's left shoulder, not serious), Corvin pulls him back, the crossbow fires wide, Vott says *enough*, gives the formal Dwarven acknowledgment, and says *Pass*.

In private, in Dwarven: Vott warns Agda specifically — 22 years at this gate, people come back different or don't come back, what's in the waystation **doesn't observe the threshold correctly**. He warns her as a threshold practitioner. She thanks him in the formal tradition response. She translates it plainly to the others and keeps the full weight to herself.

Agda tends her shoulder with *Duhr, vel thanis* (the short invocation — half the swelling, nothing miraculous). Evren holds a small lamp at the optimal angle, unprompted, without comment. Neither discusses it. They walk into the Hollowback slightly closer than usual.

### Threads advanced
- **T3 enters**: Vott's warning establishes the waystation contains something that does not respect thresholds properly. First named external confirmation.
- **T5 advances (Evren/Agda)**: She steps in front of him. He holds the light. Neither discusses either action.

input.content:
# Chapter 6 — What the Empire Paved

Two hours past Vott's gate, the canopy changed.

It happened the way the Hollowback did most of its work: incrementally, and then all at once. There was a point where Sela could have put a pin in the map and written *here the light went different.* The trees were older than the ones before, or the same age but growing closer, their canopies interlocked overhead into something that let the afternoon through in angled shafts rather than the general filtered gray of the earlier track. The shafts came down in long diagonals and hit the undergrowth at odds with themselves, and the effect was of distance simultaneously compressed and expanded — the Hollowback's specific disorientation, the one she'd been cataloging since Day 2. Twenty feet was still twenty feet. Forty felt further than forty. Certain things in the undergrowth looked closer than they were and certain things looked farther, and the difference was difficult to catch before you'd already adjusted for it.

She watched Corvin's stride change: a slight shortening, the heel coming down with a fraction more attention, the walk of a man who had decided the ground might be uneven before the ground had given him a specific reason to decide this. She shortened her own stride without deciding to and kept his pace.

Ahead of them, Evren was navigating by something she couldn't read clearly. He'd mentioned, sometime after midday, that he'd once followed a cartographer's notes in a forest like this one. He said it the way he said most things that mattered: embedded in a sentence that was mostly about something else, delivered flat and then not returned to. She'd filed it. He moved differently in these older trees — not cautious, exactly, but attentive in a way that was distinct from his usual attentiveness, which was the attentiveness of a man tracking the social geometry of a room. This was something else. An internal compass she didn't have a name for.

Agda was watching the upper canopy. Every few minutes she'd slow for two or three seconds with her head tipped back, tracking something in the branches she didn't describe. Whatever she was watching, it was consistent enough to keep watching. Sela had learned in four days to track Agda's consistent behaviors.

She opened the map and annotated the margin: *canopy changes approx. 2 hours east of checkpoint — light quality shifts, undergrowth distance unreliable.* Her master had called redundant annotation a failure of confidence. Her master had also never been in the Hollowback.

---

The track ran south-east for a while and then began to descend — not steeply, barely a slope, more a suggestion of lower ground ahead. She was watching her footing when something changed under her boot.

Not the texture of mud. Not root or embedded river stone. The word that arrived was *different* and she stopped on it before she'd finished stepping.

She planted her weight and pressed down. Underneath the mud, underneath the root-work and the three centuries of accumulated leaf-fall and whatever the forest floor became when nothing disturbed it long enough: flat. Not incidentally flat, not the flat of a smooth stone worn by water. Geometrically flat. In a way that forest floors were not, in a way that nothing shaped by water or time and left alone was.

She stopped walking.

Behind her: Evren's footsteps stopped, then Agda's, then Corvin's from slightly left — always the edge of the group.

Sela crouched. Pressed her boot toe against the mud and scraped, slowly, until the edge of something came clear. She worked her fingernail into the gap between it and whatever was beside it. The join was tight enough that she couldn't get the nail in past the quick.

Stone. Not local limestone — the Hollowback's natural outcroppings tended toward a warm gray with faint orange vein. This was darker and harder, and the surface where the mud had come away was cut flat with a precision that implied a process she couldn't have described but could recognize as intentional. As designed.

"The road," Evren said. Quietly, from just behind her. Not a question.

She straightened. Looked east along the track's descent, then up at the canopy, then at the map, though she already knew what the map would confirm. The Vethic roads had run through this forest before it was this forest, before the empire that built them had stopped. She'd known that. It was in the survey fragments, in the history, in the pencil line on Maret's map that Ren had extended past his sister's notation into the blank eastern space. She'd known it as information and had not quite expected it to be *here*, under her boot, exactly as flat as the day it was laid.

"The forest grew over it," she said. "Not through it."

---

They followed the road east for a quarter mile.

In some places the mud was thin enough that the paving came almost visible — the dark, fitted stone in rows, the joins between each piece so even they read as a single surface at distance. In other places root-work had heaved the paving up, centuries of pressure finding the seams and working them, but never breaking the geometry underneath. She could feel it through her boots even where she couldn't see it: the flat beneath the uneven, the road insisting on itself below three hundred years of forest floor.

At the crest of a low rise the track curved away from the old road's surface to avoid a cluster of old growth, and ahead of the curve a long strip of Vethic stone came up where the roots hadn't reached — clean, or close to clean, moss-covered but level, running toward a gap in the canopy where some large tree had fallen long enough ago that the secondary growth hadn't closed the opening. The afternoon light came through the gap at a broad angle and hit the rise.

She stopped at the crest without making a decision to stop.

The light fell on the paving stones. And the stones — the moss on the stones, the air a hand's breadth above the surface of the stones — did something she did not have an immediate word for. Not glow. Not bioluminescence. Not light produced from within. It was the particular effect of old stone releasing warmth: three centuries of heat, accumulated and slow, radiating into the cooler air of a forest afternoon, the differential so slight it was barely there and so consistent it was entirely there — a shimmer, just at the edge of visible, the air above the road's surface trembling fractionally between colder and warmer.

Like the road was still warm. Like something that wide and that old and that precisely made took a very long time to cool, and three hundred years had not been enough.

She stood on it. Solid under her boots — the road didn't move, didn't flex, didn't give. She looked down the long pale strip toward the light gap and then back along the road's line into the deep dark where the old canopy closed, and something happened in her chest that she did not have a name for and did not try to find one.

The road was built by people who expected it to last. It did. They didn't.

---

Nobody made a joke.

That was the first thing she took stock of, and she took stock of it fully: Evren, who filled silences with the efficiency of something that had been designed to fill them, was not filling this one. He'd crouched at the near end of the exposed pavement and was running two fingers slowly along the join between stones, both fingers pressing as if testing whether the fit would give. His face had the specific expression she'd come to identify over four days as the one he wore when he was actually present — when there was nothing being performed, because nothing that warranted performance was happening. He was just looking at the join.

Agda had moved to the crest of the rise and stood with her arms at her sides, not touching anything, looking back along the road the direction it must have run before the trees had it — the dark closing of the old canopy, the place where the sightline died. She had the still quality she got when she was doing something internally that she would not be explaining.

Corvin had not crouched. He stood on the pavement beside Sela, and after a long moment — the kind of moment that had a texture, that you could feel the edges of — he said, very quietly: "Three hundred years."

And then nothing else.

The nothing else had a weight to it. She would keep the nothing else.

---

The road descended from the crest into a section where the root-work had heaved the original drainage channels entirely, and the low ground beyond had filled — not deep water, but enough that the old paving was submerged and the soil on either side had the particular softness of ground that didn't dry fully. She checked the map. The Vethic road ran in the right direction, but the right direction required that section to be passable, and it was not passable with the afternoon already moving toward evening.

"We go back to the track," she said.

Nobody argued. They picked their way off the rise and retraced the quarter mile to where the track forked, the road's surface slipping back under the forest floor as they went. The track rejoined the road's alignment farther east, shallower, the stone occasional and less spectacular — a surface that asserted itself in flashes and then went under again. She made a note of where it surfaced and where it didn't, correlating it to the map's existing annotations.

The track doubled back once without apparent reason, running northwest for a stretch before correcting northeast. She noted that too. It was the kind of thing that ate time, the Hollowback's specific discourtesy.

Evren came up alongside her. At intervals since mid-morning he'd moved to a position adjacent rather than behind, which was not his usual arrangement in the group's loose formation. She'd attributed the first shift to the track's topography. She'd stopped attributing it and started simply noting it.

He walked without saying anything for a while. Long enough that she'd stopped expecting him to say anything.

"The cartographer I mentioned," he said.

She waited.

"She said the road would still be there."

The pronoun landed with a specific weight. She felt its weight and noticed she was feeling it and noticed him stopping — the sentence ending there, no additional clause, nothing appended to soften or explain or deflect. The sentence contained exactly and only what he had put in it.

"She was right," Sela said.

"Yes."

He moved back to his position behind and slightly right sometime after that. She did not track him moving.

---

The campsite Corvin chose was in a hollow where a large tree had come down, opening a gap in the understory long enough ago that the ground was firm rather than churned. Good sightlines northwest and east, the direction they'd come from and the direction they were heading. The unlocatable water sound was present to the south, which made it slightly more locatable than usual, which didn't mean she'd find it.

Agda built the fire. She did it with the economy of someone who had done this alone before — the sequence of kindling and feed arranged without discussion, committed to a configuration before she'd finished assessing the materials. Not a fire for company. A fire that would hold its temperature and be manageable, built without ceremony and without waste.

Sela ate, and read.

The fragments Maret had given her occupied three pages in Ren's handwriting — passed across the desk in the warehouse office with the air of something being surrendered that had been kept private. They were not a narrative. Survey observations, measurements rendered in Vethic notation with a student's hand — confident in some marks and working through uncertainty in others — and a sketch of what might be the outer gateway configuration, the proportions slightly off but the orientation clear. She'd been reading them since Thorngate. She still couldn't read the Vethic notation fluently, but she could see the logic of the system, the grammar of it, a notation built for precision and speed by people for whom precision and speed had mattered.

He was nineteen. He'd taught himself — or been taught — to read a notational system that most professional cartographers treated as historical curiosity and most other people treated as decorative marking on old stone. He'd walked into the Hollowback alone, with a belt knife and a double-buckled satchel, and his note to his sister had been four words of reassurance that implied he'd been not afraid, or afraid in a way that had not been enough to stop him.

She found this interesting. She put the pages away.

Across the fire, Evren had produced the two pieces of the broken crossbow and was working through some adjustment to the trigger mechanism she couldn't see clearly from her angle. She'd stopped tracking this as strange — it was simply something he did in the evenings now, the way Corvin sharpened his knife, a settling-in behavior. Agda was waxing a section of her kit's leather strap, the cloth moving in the same small repetitive arc, her attention somewhere that wasn't the camp.

Corvin had taken up his watch position: a tree at the camp's northeast edge with a clear angle on the track. The knife sound, steel on stone, started and stopped at intervals. Background information. Present and useful.

She slept.

---

In the dark, she woke.

Not to a sound. To the end of a sound — the particular quality of air after something has moved through it, the way stillness returns to itself slightly wrong when something has just left. She lay without moving and read the forest.

The fire had banked to coals. The temperature had dropped. Someone had taken watch; she hadn't been woken for the change, which meant it was somewhere past midnight, deep enough into the night that whoever was watching had made the call to let her sleep. She made a note of it and did not move.

East of the camp, at the edge of the flat ground where the understory began: a sound.

Not the movement of something large. Animals in the Hollowback moved in ways she'd been cataloging: the crashing scale of something bigger than the undergrowth, or the long cautious pause of something that had caught a scent and stopped. The wood settling — the old trees made that sound under their own weight, long slow cracks in the cold that traveled differently than anything alive. The unlocatable water. She had sounds sorted.

This was not sorted.

It was the sound of something moving carefully. Step by deliberate step, each placement chosen, the movement of something that knew it was near a camp and was making decisions about its feet accordingly. It came from one direction and then stopped. Not because it had moved out of range. Because it had stopped.

She lay still. The coals gave a faint orange light that reached nowhere useful.

The Hollowback made sounds. She knew this. The water carried other sounds across distance in unpredictable ways, the forest was old enough that its own shifts and settlings mimicked things she didn't want to name. She knew these things.

She lay in the dark and listened to the stillness coming back to itself and told herself: an animal. Something that hunts carefully, that pauses when it catches a camp's edge. Something with an instinct for caution that read, from here, like intention.

She almost believed it.

The sound didn't return. She lay awake for a while listening to it not returning, and after a while the Hollowback settled into its ordinary background of water and old wood, and she slept again.

In the morning she wrote nothing in the map margin about the sound.

input.oneLine: Sela finds the Vethic road still flat under three centuries of forest floor; nobody makes a joke; Evren says *she* and stops; something moves at the camp edge with intention.

input.summary: Sela's POV, Day 4 afternoon into night. Two hours past Vott's gate, the canopy deepens and the Hollowback's distance-distortion effect intensifies. Evren navigates by something internal; Agda watches the upper canopy; Corvin's stride shortens; Sela annotates the map. On a descending section of track, Sela's boot finds geometrically flat stone under the mud and root-work — the Vethic paving, still exact, the forest grown over it rather than through it. They follow the exposed road surface east for a quarter mile. At the crest of a low rise with a gap in the canopy, the afternoon light falls on the Vethic paving stones and the air above shimmers slightly — old stone releasing stored heat into cooler forest air, barely-visible, entirely real. Sela stands on it and something happens in her chest she does not name. Nobody makes a joke — Evren crouches tracing the join between stones, face actually present; Agda stands at the crest looking back down the road's line; Corvin says quietly 'Three hundred years' and nothing else, and the nothing else sounds like awe. The road descends into a flooded section with collapsed drainage; they return to the track. Evren moves alongside Sela for a stretch and says: 'The cartographer I mentioned — she said the road would still be there.' One pronoun. He stops. She says 'She was right.' He says 'Yes.' He moves back to his position. Camp in a hollow where a fallen tree opened the understory — Agda builds the fire with the economy of someone who has camped alone, Sela re-reads Ren's three-page working notes (survey observations, Vethic notation, a gateway sketch Maret gave her). She thinks about a nineteen-year-old who taught himself to read Vethic notation and walked in alone. Corvin takes watch. In the deep night Sela wakes to the end of a sound and then hears something east of camp moving with deliberate, placed steps — the movement of something that knows it is near a camp and is choosing its footfalls. It stops. Not because it moved away. She tells herself it's an animal and almost believes it. In the morning she writes nothing in the map margin about the sound. Threads advanced: T2 (Evren's female pronoun — 'she said the road would still be there' — one word and a stop); T3 (the night movement — deliberate, intentional, not animal-typical); T5 Sela/Corvin second notable beat ('three hundred years' + the nothing else + the shared road surface).
newFacts: The Vethic road through the Hollowback: geometrically flat paving stone survives intact under the forest floor. The join between stones is tight enough that a fingernail cannot be inserted past the quick. The stone is darker and harder than local Hollowback limestone (which is warm gray with faint orange vein). The forest grew over it, not through it.

The Vethic paving stones release three centuries of stored heat into cooler forest air, producing a barely-visible shimmer in the air just above the road surface in afternoon light — not glow, not bioluminescence, the differential temperature effect of very old stone releasing warmth slowly. This is the moment of awe.

The Vethic road's surface is intermittently exposed for approximately a quarter mile east of where the party finds it. At the crest of a low rise, a gap in the canopy allows afternoon light to fall on the exposed paving. Past the crest, the road descends into a flooded section where root-work has collapsed the original drainage channels; the pavement there is submerged and the surrounding ground waterlogged — impassable by the party's route to the waystation.

Ren Ashford's working notes (distinct from his departure note): three pages in his handwriting, given to Sela by Maret at the warehouse office. Contents: survey observations, measurements in Vethic notation (student hand — confident in some marks, uncertain in others), and a sketch of what is probably the outer gateway configuration with proportions slightly off but orientation clear. Sela cannot read the Vethic notation fluently but can infer its logic.

Evren said to Sela on Day 4 afternoon, walking adjacent alongside her on the track: 'The cartographer I mentioned — she said the road would still be there.' He stopped at the female pronoun. Sela said 'She was right.' He said 'Yes.' He did not add anything further. This is the first explicit gendering of the cartographer Evren mentioned in relation to his prior knowledge.

Corvin said, quietly, while standing on the exposed Vethic paving at the crest of the rise: 'Three hundred years.' He said nothing else. Sela cataloged the nothing else as sounding like awe in his register and said she would keep it.

Nobody in the party made a joke at the awe moment on the Vethic road — not even Evren. Sela registered this as significant. Evren crouched and traced the join between paving stones; Agda stood at the crest looking back along the road's line; Corvin stood beside Sela.
Day 4 evening campsite: a hollow where a large old tree had fallen, ground firm, understory open enough for sightlines northwest and east.

Agda builds camp fires with the economy of someone who has camped alone before — no discussion, no ceremony, configuration committed before the materials are fully assessed. The fire holds its temperature and is built to be manageable, not impressive.

Something moved east of the Day 4 camp in the deep night: deliberate, placed steps, each footfall chosen, the movement of something that knew it was near a camp and was making decisions about its feet accordingly. It stopped without moving out of range. Sela heard only the end of the sound and then the stillness returning. She told herself it was an animal and almost believed it.
Sela did not cast any magic in Chapter 6. She is conserving. Her headache from Ch04 is entirely gone by Day 4.
The track on Day 4 doubled back northwest once before correcting northeast — a misdirection Sela noted in the map margin.

Evren's standard position in the party's loose traveling formation: behind and slightly right of the main line. On Day 4 he moved to an adjacent alongside position at intervals, moving back to his standard position after the cartographer pronoun exchange.
looseThreads: T2 — The Cartographer Who Didn't Come Back: Evren has now said 'she' in front of Sela. One pronoun and a stop. Sela noticed and did not ask. The cartographer is now confirmed female to the reader; not yet named, relationship not disclosed. Pays off in Ch07 when her name (Lira) appears in Evren's interior.

T3 — The Thing the Empire Left Running: the night movement on Day 4 is ambiguous — it could be something connected to the Hollowed One's influence at range, or the forest, or a scout of some kind. Left unresolved. Not a named threat. The forest is saying: you are not the first things to have been here.

T5 — The Argument Against Distance, Sela/Corvin: 'Three hundred years' and the nothing-else are the second notable beat of this pairing. They stood on the road together. They moved at the same pace on the track for a stretch without speaking. Neither named anything. The reader carries it.
Ren's working notes: Sela cannot read the Vethic notation fluently. She can see the logic. This will matter when Evren reads it aloud in Ch08.

The flooded section of the Vethic road: the party could not use the road directly toward the waystation due to the collapsed drainage. The road's geometry holds but the route is blocked. May have bearing on the approach to the waystation exterior in Ch07.

facts: The Vethic road through the Hollowback survives intact under the forest floor. The paving stones are geometrically flat, joined so tightly a fingernail cannot be inserted past the quick. The stone is darker and harder than local Hollowback limestone (warm gray with faint orange vein). The forest grew over the road, not through it. Surface is intermittently exposed for approximately a quarter mile in the section found on Day 4.

The Vethic paving stones produce a barely-visible shimmer in the air above their surface in afternoon light: old stone releasing three centuries of stored warmth into cooler forest air. Not glow, not bioluminescence — a temperature differential effect. This is the moment of awe established in Ch06.

Past the crest of the low rise where the party found the exposed road, the Vethic road descends into a flooded section where root-work has collapsed the original drainage. The paving there is submerged; surrounding ground is waterlogged. The party could not use this route to continue toward the waystation and returned to the track.

Ren Ashford's working notes (distinct from his departure note and the map): three pages in his handwriting, given to Sela by Maret at the warehouse office. Contents: survey observations, measurements in Vethic notation (student hand), and a sketch of what appears to be the outer gateway configuration. Sela cannot read the Vethic notation fluently but can infer the logic of the system.

Evren said to Sela on Day 4 afternoon: 'The cartographer I mentioned — she said the road would still be there.' He stopped at the female pronoun and added nothing else. This is the first explicit female gendering of the cartographer whose survey notes Evren found three years ago. Her name is not yet disclosed (established as Lira in Ch07 interior only).

Corvin said quietly while standing on the exposed Vethic paving at the crest of the low rise: 'Three hundred years.' He said nothing else. This is the second notable beat of the Sela/Corvin romance thread.

Something moved east of the Day 4 camp in the deep night: deliberate, placed footfalls, the movement of something choosing each step with awareness of the camp's proximity. It stopped without moving out of range. Sela heard the end of the sound and the stillness returning. Ambiguous — possibly connected to the Hollowed One's influence at range, possibly the forest. Not named, not resolved.
Day 4 evening campsite: a hollow where an old tree fell, ground firm, understory open. Sightlines northwest and east.
Sela cast no magic in Ch06. Her headache from Ch04 is fully resolved by Day 4. She is conserving.
Evren's standard formation position in the party: behind and slightly right. On Day 4 he moved alongside Sela at intervals before returning to his standard position.

entries.definition: The paved road built by the Vethic Empire through what is now the Hollowback forest. Survives intact under three centuries of forest floor — geometrically flat stone, joins so tight a fingernail cannot be inserted, darker and harder than local limestone. The forest grew over it rather than through it. Intermittently visible where roots have not covered it. Produces a barely-visible thermal shimmer in afternoon light (stored heat releasing into cooler air). Descends into a flooded, impassable section east of the low rise where the party found it in Ch06.

Three pages of field notes in Ren Ashford's handwriting, given to Sela by Maret at the warehouse office. Contain survey observations, measurements in Vethic notation (student hand, confident in some marks), and a sketch of what appears to be the outer gateway configuration of the Eastmark Waystation. Distinct from Ren's departure note and from the hand-annotated map in Maret's office. Sela carries these throughout the journey.

## Chapter 6 — What the Empire Paved: Production Summary

**Draft:** `draft/06-what-the-empire-paved.md` (~2,900 words, Sela POV)

**What the chapter does:**

The chapter is the outlined breathing beat between the charter confrontation and the waystation. It moves in six movements:

1. **The changed canopy** — two hours past Vott's gate, the older trees and angled light make the Hollowback's distance-distortion worse. Each party member responds in character: Corvin shortens his stride; Evren navigates by an internal compass Sela can't read; Agda watches the upper canopy; Sela annotates the map.

2. **The road reasserts itself** — under Sela's boot, through mud and three centuries of root-work, the Vethic paving: geometrically flat, the join between stones too tight for a fingernail. Evren names it quietly. "The forest grew over it. Not through it."

3. **The moment of awe** (brief's required beat, earned) — at the crest of a low rise where a canopy gap lets in afternoon light, the old stone releases stored warmth into the cooler air and the surface shimmers barely, entirely. Old, precise, still warm. Sela's chest does something she doesn't name. The road was built to last. It did. They didn't.

4. **The party, briefly** — nobody makes a joke (Sela registers this as significant). Evren crouches and traces the join, actually present. Agda looks back along the road's vanishing line. Corvin says *three hundred years* and nothing else. Sela keeps the nothing else.

5. **The pronoun** — on the track east, Evren moves alongside Sela and says: *The cartographer I mentioned — she said the road would still be there.* One pronoun. He stops. Sela says "She was right." He says "Yes." He moves back to his position. Thread T2 advanced; Sela does not ask.

6. **Camp and the sound** — Agda builds the fire with practiced economy; Sela re-reads Ren's working notes and thinks about a nineteen-year-old who taught himself Vethic notation. In the deep night: deliberate, placed steps east of camp — something that knows it is near people and is choosing its feet. It stops without moving away. Sela almost believes it's an animal.

**Threads advanced:** T2 (Evren's "she"), T3 (night movement — intentional, ambiguous), T5 Sela/Corvin (second notable beat).

input.content:
# Chapter 7 — Watch

The fire was coals. Not even particularly good coals — the kind that had done their work and were now doing the slow second work of becoming ash. There was enough red in them to be company. There was not enough to see past the tree line.

Evren sat with his back against a birch he'd tested for loose bark before committing to it, because loose bark makes sound and sound on watch is information the watch has just handed over. The others were asleep. Agda had her back to a different tree; Sela had her cloak tucked up around her ears in the configuration she'd arrived at on Day 2 that looked uncomfortable and apparently wasn't; Corvin was lying with the purposeful stillness of someone who could be vertical in two seconds. Four and a half feet from Evren's knife hand to Corvin's left elbow. He'd measured it when he sat down.

He always measured it. He measured the distance from his knife hand to the nearest ally the way he measured the distance to every exit. The same way he'd been sitting with his back to a tree where he could see in three directions since the second night on the road — back always to something solid, the angles covered, the camp's approach in his sightline. He did this without thinking. The same way Corvin always took the position nearest the door or nearest the track.

He did not always think about where that habit came from. He was thinking about it tonight.

---

The Hollowback at night was not quiet. That was the first thing a person got wrong, and Evren had gotten it wrong himself, once, when he was considerably younger and more willing to name the dark as an absence rather than a presence of different things. Water, east of the camp — the same unlocatable run of it that had been there since they passed through Vott's gate — flowing over something or around something at a pitch that said it was not slow. The trees settling: old wood under its own weight, the long low creak of something not in distress but large, the sound of very old things being very old. Night birds trading calls across the canopy in the particular rhythmic delay that meant they were far away and attending to each other. A boar west of them, going away, until it was past the camp's radius.

He had the Hollowback's night register down now. Three days of data, which was not a word he used out loud, but which was accurate.

Which was why he was thinking about Lira.

She'd said: *the oldest forests hold the sounds differently than the scrubland — the distances shift, you have to recalibrate.* The second night he'd walked point, two years before the Ashford contract, in a situation that was not this situation but had been instructive. He'd said something facetious. She'd kept reading her notes without looking up. He had recalibrated.

The survey notes had been in a stack of discarded paper in the back of a cartographer's office on Medway Lane. Three years ago, two months after she'd stopped being reachable. He'd been in the back of the office because the front of the office had nothing useful, and he found most things he needed by not being where anyone expected him to be. The notes had had corners browned with age and had stopped mid-page mid-sentence. He'd read them in the alley. By the time he got back to his room he had them memorized — the approach road, the junction configuration, the outer gateway stonework, and then the sentence that ended in the middle of a measurement and didn't start again.

She had gone to the waystation to verify the survey. She had not come back.

He was here to find out why. The Ashford contract had given him a path inside without requiring him to explain his reasons, which had been convenient enough that he'd been briefly suspicious of the convenience. The convenience had held. Four days in, twenty kilometers out, apparently exactly where the survey notes said it would be.

He had not told the party this. He had been doing the inventory on whether to tell the party since Day 1, and the inventory was getting harder to justify in either direction. In one direction: he had a personal reason for being in this specific ruin, he had information about the waystation he hadn't shared, and the not-sharing was beginning to feel like lying by arrangement. In the other direction: Corvin saw things he didn't want seen and kept them to himself, which was a kind of courtesy Evren recognized and hadn't expected to receive. Sela was filing things she hadn't asked about, which was a different kind of courtesy. And Agda—

The coals shifted. A small sound. Nothing alarming.

He had been thinking about Agda a lot, on watch. This was new information about himself. He was choosing not to look at it directly, the way you chose not to look at a light source when you needed to keep your night vision.

---

The sound came from east-northeast.

Not the boar. Not the water. Not the long settling of old wood. It was weight-bearing and deliberate — the rhythm of something placing each foot, the movement of something that knew it was near a camp and was making choices about its steps accordingly. It came from where the firelight gave up and the understory began to matter, and it stopped.

His hand was on his knife before he'd processed that his hand was on his knife.

He waited. Counted: one to twenty, then again. The forest went on being the forest around the not-sound where the thing had been — the water, the canopy birds, the distant wood creak. He sat with the knife against his palm and tracked the direction — east-northeast, no farther than thirty meters — and held it, and at five minutes the silence had settled back into the regular kind.

He let go of the knife. He did not look away from east-northeast.

The same thing Sela had woken to. He knew it from the way she hadn't mentioned it at morning, which was the same way he wouldn't mention it: the sound had stopped, the forest had gone on. You said nothing because saying it gave it a weight it might not have earned. You noted the bearing and kept watching.

He noted the bearing and kept watching.

---

Agda woke sometime in the small hours that had no particular hour to them, just the quality of the night at its most resolved. She came up from sleep the way she did everything else: without preamble, the transition from lying down to sitting upright in one motion, like someone had said her name at the threshold of her hearing and she'd answered.

She looked at him.

He had been about to make a joke. He did not make the joke.

In the dark, at this distance, with only coals for light, she should not have been able to read his face clearly. She was reading it anyway. This had been happening since Day 1 and was not becoming less unsettling with repetition.

She got up. Came to sit near the coals — close enough that a conversation wouldn't require raising voices, not close enough to be asking for one. She picked up a stick and adjusted something in the coals, and a thin warmth came back from the red beneath.

The forest went on.

"You know more about where we're going," Agda said, "than you've said."

Not a question. Delivered with the same flatness she delivered everything she was certain of — no edge, no *and*, no pressure underneath. A statement, arrived at fully formed from some process he hadn't been shown, set between them on the ground like a tool she'd decided to put down.

He had the deflection ready. Standard inventory — two or three options, all functional, deployable in under a second. He could feel their shapes from here.

She was watching him have the deflection ready.

He put it down.

"Some," he said.

She nodded once. She looked at the coals. She did not follow up with anything.

He sat with the knowledge that she could have. Any of six natural directions from here; he'd have had to work against all of them. She'd chosen not to, and the choice was visible, and the visible choice was a kind of trust extended without being demanded. He had received trust in his life. He had generally found it alarming.

This was more alarming than most.

---

They sat by the fire.

He did not make a single joke. This was not unprecedented as a circumstance — there were rooms and moments where the mechanism had the good sense to know the joke would land wrong. But those were circumstances. Agda was a different category. She simply sat there, not asking for anything, not performing patience, and the jokes, which were usually available in a constant ambient way like background weather, were not available.

He tried to remember the last time this had been true. He was fourteen, and it had been a different situation entirely, and he was not going to think about it on watch in the Hollowback.

Twenty minutes. He would have said fifteen if anyone asked.

At some point Agda's breathing went long and even — the depth of someone asleep — and she was still upright where she'd been, head tipped slightly forward, eyes closed. He had heard at some point that dwarves could do this. He had several questions, but they would keep.

He watched the three directions. The water ran east of them.

He sat for another hour with something he didn't have a word for and would greatly prefer not to examine, and the warmth from the coals came to him, and the night went on.

---

The sky went from black to the gray that came before the gray that came before dawn. He had learned to read these distinctions years ago for reasons he no longer remembered, and he'd retained them the way he retained most things that might someday be useful: involuntarily.

He woke Corvin first. Two fingers on the shoulder; Corvin was upright in a second. No words needed. Corvin had the watch assessed and taken before Evren had fully stood, which was the thing about Corvin — operational before awake, or possibly never not operational, it was difficult to determine.

Agda woke herself. The others were coming up from sleep in the ordinary sequence. Sela had that look she got in the first few minutes of consciousness, the one that resolved in about four minutes into her standard expression as if a process were completing.

Evren walked the perimeter.

He did this every morning. Half habit, half the professional opinion that things you didn't check were the things that bit you. A loose circuit of the camp's edge: northwest, east, south, back. He'd been doing it since the first camp; nobody had commented.

He was twenty meters into the trees on the northeast side when he found it.

The wrapping was Thorngate. Not a guess — the cord around it was the two-ply that Thorngate traders used when buying from Millhaven provisioners, the short knotted end he'd seen in a hundred market stalls. The oilcloth had the slight yellow tint of Millhaven rendering that made its way to Thorngate by the yard. Three weeks, give or take; the cord had gone soft with moisture but the oilcloth had kept its integrity. Whatever food had been in it was long gone without the packaging having been pulled apart by anything looking. Someone had eaten their provisions here and left the wrapping. Someone who knew how to leave a site.

Four stones in a cairn beside a root, flat-faced and balanced, each one chosen for the flatness of its face and stacked with a precision that wasn't accident. A bearing marker, provisional, left by someone who knew they were coming back or who wanted the heading available on the return.

He straightened and found the birch.

White bark, at eye height — the only kind of tree you used for this, because the color held the mark. The notation was cut shallow, still pale at the cut edges, which put it at three weeks and no more. The instrument had been a belt knife rather than a surveyor's blade; the line thickness went uneven at the joints in the specific way of someone who knew the system and didn't have the right tool.

He read it.

A bearing marker. Eastern heading, corrected for the magnetic deviation the Vethic engineers had surveyed and noted in this section of the forest — a degree and a half north if you didn't account for it, which would put you at the wrong section of the waystation's approach road and cost you time you might not have. Ren had known to account for it. Ren had been confirming his heading here three weeks ago, leaving a reference he might want on the return.

The bearing was the same correction Evren had been running in his own head since they'd passed the deviation zone the previous afternoon. He had not mentioned the deviation zone.

He looked up.

Sela was watching him. Corvin had moved slightly left — he'd been watching and had looked away before Evren turned, which was, actually, worse. Agda had gone still near the fire.

Four seconds. He had not paused at the script. He had not sounded it out or worked through its logic; he had read it with the immediacy of his own language, comprehension without effort.

"Old cartographer's trick," he said. "You pick it up."

Sela said nothing. She had the expression she used when she'd filed something and was not commenting on it. Whatever she'd filed was not going anywhere.

He had never once picked up anything from a cartographer. He had learned Vethic survey notation because Lira had taught him — letter by letter, across the back of a survey sheet she'd been about to discard, two years before the Ashford contract. She had said: *you'll want this if you ever follow someone's notes into the field.* Not a prediction. A practical assessment, delivered without looking up, the same way she made all her practical assessments.

He had said something in response. He didn't remember what. She'd kept working. He'd had the system memorized by afternoon.

"We're close," he said.

"A day," Corvin said.

"Less, if the track holds."

Nobody argued. Agda handed him something hot in a camp cup without being asked, without comment, and he wrapped his hands around it and watched the light come up through the old canopy in the pale angled way the Hollowback had for morning, and Ren's four stones sat in their precise stack in the root-mulch, and the notation was right there in the birch bark, and the bearing was correct.

Day 5.

Evren reads Vethic survey notation fluently and without hesitation — established in front of the full party in Ch07. He read Ren's birch-tree notation in approximately four seconds.
